subject: [PATCH 03/10] nfsd: serialize nfsd4_end_grace() with atomic test-and-set

reviews.author: Chuck Lever
reviews.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever suggested using an atomic bit operation instead of try_cmpxchg() to ensure consistent behavior across all hardware platforms.
reviews.raw_body:
Seems like the usual idiom for something like this is an atomic
bit op. Perhaps try_cmpxchg on a boolean variable is not going
to behave as you expect on every hardware platform.

-- 
Chuck Lever

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: Author responded to concerns about the use of try_cmpxchg() by stating that it should work the same way on every platform, implying that the patch is correct and no further revision is needed.
sentiment_signals: author expressed confidence in the patch's correctness
no indication of a need for further revision
reviews.raw_body:
We just need a single flag here though. try_cmpxchg() had better work
the same way on every platform or a lot of stuff is FUBAR. Where
wouldn't it?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed a concern about concurrent access to nfsd_net's bool variables, agreed to convert them to an unsigned long flag variable and restructure in the next version.
reviews.raw_body:
Ok, I'm convinced. What I think I'll do is add a new flags unsigned
long to struct nfsd_net and we can convert most of the bools in that
struct to use it instead.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

2026-05-29.patch_summary: This patch serializes the nfsd4_end_grace() function by replacing a plain bool read/write pair with an atomic test-and-set operation using try_cmpxchg(). This ensures that only one caller can transition the grace_ended flag from false to true, preventing concurrent walkers from corrupting the reclaim_str_hashtbl list and double-freeing nfs4_client_reclaim entries. The patch addresses a concurrency issue in the NFS daemon's laundromat path and RECLAIM_COMPLETE path, where multiple threads could observe the grace period as not ended and proceed into the drain, leading to data corruption.
